articles.title: A New Complexity Theory for the Quantum Age
articles.summary: Henry Yuen (Columbia) is building a new mathematical framework for "fully quantum" complexity theory -- where problems can have quantum inputs and outputs, not classical bit strings. Traditional P vs. NP-style complexity theory assumes everything in and out is a string of bits; Yuen showed in 2020 (co-proving MIP* = RE) that quantum entanglement breaks the classical hierarchy in shocking ways, and argues this demands an entirely new theory of computational hardness built from the ground up.
takeaways: The MIP* = RE result means quantum entanglement in multi-prover interactive proofs can verify problems far beyond classical recursion -- an unexpected collapse that signals classical complexity theory cannot handle quantum systems
There exists a whole class of quantum-input/quantum-output problems that complexity theory has barely touched; Yuen is building the mathematical language to even describe them
Constructing a fully quantum complexity theory requires rethinking what it means for a problem to have a well-defined input or solution -- not just adding quantum gates to classical frameworks

articles.title: Escaping the Ogallala Trap
articles.summary: The Ogallala Aquifer -- beneath 111 million acres of US farmland -- has lost 286 million acre-feet since electrified irrigation began in the 1940s, equivalent to draining Lake Erie. Large parts of western Kansas and Texas are 20-30 years from exhaustion. Ben Southwood uses this tragedy of the commons as a direct analogy for the road network: autonomous vehicles will make driving frictionless, massively increasing trips, and unless road pricing is implemented before AVs become politically entrenched, we face the same collective-ruin dynamic.
takeaways: Aquifer depletion is a canonical tragedy of the commons -- each farmer's rational pumping contributes to collective destruction with no individual actor able to stop it unilaterally
Electric and autonomous vehicles reduce the marginal cost of a trip toward zero, which is structurally identical to the free-pumping dynamic that destroyed the Ogallala
Singapore and London show congestion pricing works; the political window to implement it is now, before AV fleets create a constituency of sunk costs and path-dependent opposition
The piece makes the novel case that road pricing isn't just about congestion -- it's about preserving the commons before it gets locked open

articles.title: Microbubbles
articles.summary: Microscopic gas-filled shells can be loaded with drugs, injected into the bloodstream, and detonated precisely at target tissues by an external ultrasound pulse -- including across the blood-brain barrier, which blocks roughly 98% of all therapeutic molecules. Used in cardiac imaging since the 1990s, microbubbles remain unapproved for drug delivery despite decades of promising results. Ambika Grover explains the regulatory double-bind and what the path to clinical translation looks like.
takeaways: Ultrasound-driven cavitation -- the controlled collapse of a microbubble -- creates a brief pressure pulse that temporarily forces open tight junctions in biological barriers like the blood-brain barrier
The approval problem is a double-bind: regulators must assess whether the bubble reached its target AND whether the carried drug worked, making attribution of failure unusually hard
Therapeutic bubbles are furthest along for brain tumors, where disease severity shifts the risk calculus sharply toward intervention
A 2025 Nature Physics paper on cyclic jetting showed a more controlled cavitation mechanism, suggesting the underlying biophysics is still being worked out at the frontier

articles.title: The Man Who Stole Infinity
articles.summary: Georg Cantor's 1874 proof of different-sized infinities is canonical mathematical history -- but a newly unearthed cache of letters between Cantor and Richard Dedekind shows that the key conceptual moves were Dedekind's, and Cantor incorporated them without attribution after their private correspondence. Part of Quanta's new series on the evolving foundations of mathematics.
takeaways: Dedekind had independently developed the concept of bijection between infinite sets -- the engine that makes Cantor's hierarchy of infinities work -- before Cantor's 1874 paper appeared
The letters show Cantor asking Dedekind increasingly specific foundational questions and then publishing without credit; Dedekind noticed but said little publicly
Mathematical priority claims are persistently distorted by the lone-genius narrative; collaborative intellectual debts routinely get erased in canonical histories
This is part of Quanta's series on foundations of math -- companions to the digitized-proofs piece already in the reading list

articles.title: A Buddhist Sun Miracle?
articles.summary: In September 1998, 20,000 Buddhists at the Dhammakaya Temple in Bangkok reported seeing a miraculous vision in the sun after collective meditation -- strikingly similar to the Miracle of Fatima in 1917 where Catholic crowds reported the sun dancing. Scott Alexander argues this cross-religious replication, stripped of Catholic iconography, suggests sun miracles are a real repeatable perceptual phenomenon, and that most scientific dismissals fail to engage with the phenomenology seriously.
takeaways: The form of the vision was invariant across traditions -- a brilliant disc in motion -- while the content was culturally specific (Catholics saw Mary; Buddhists saw their sect's founder), suggesting a real perceptual substrate interpreted through religious schemas
Dhammakaya meditation involves visualizing a glowing sphere inside the body, which may prime practitioners to project a luminous disc onto the retinal afterimage of the sun
The replication in an independent non-Catholic context is the crux: Fatima could be mass hysteria, but two independent traditions producing the same visual structure is harder to dismiss
A model of how to take religious phenomenology seriously as empirical data without either dismissing it or endorsing supernatural explanations

articles.title: The Moral Risk of Prediction Markets for Weather Catastrophes
articles.summary: As prediction markets for wildfires, hurricanes, and other catastrophes have grown into a multi-billion-dollar industry, a philosopher examines whether holding large financial positions on catastrophic outcomes creates a novel form of structural moral hazard -- not the insurer's traditional moral hazard, but the inverse: actors who profit from disaster. Arizona's AG filed criminal charges against Kalshi in March 2026; the legal and ethical status of these markets remains genuinely unsettled.
takeaways: Traditional moral hazard involves the insured party taking more risk; prediction markets create the structural opposite -- bettors with financial incentives for bad outcomes to materialize

The rationalist celebration of prediction markets as pure information aggregation ignores who participates, what positions they hold, and whether large positions create subtle incentives against mitigation
The disaster capitalism framing and the prediction market framing have never been seriously confronted with each other -- this piece attempts that collision
Arizona's criminal charges against Kalshi and new federal legislation in 2026 show the legal consensus around these markets is fragile and rapidly evolving
